mainText:
**New Mexico’s Bet on Universal Child Care**
On November 1, Democratic Governor Michelle Lujan Grisham of New Mexico launched a long-anticipated universal child care program. Last month, Grisham signed SB241 into law, which codifies this initiative and secures funding for the program through 2031.
Under this program, children from households of any income can attend child care centers at no cost. Funding will flow directly from the state to the providers; some families may incur copays in the future, but facilities are prohibited from charging fees for registration, educational activities, or transportation, except for field trips.
New Mexico does not operate child care centers itself nor assigns children to particular facilities. Instead, parents may select from over 1,000 participating child care programs that are either private or nonprofit organizations, including alternative centers that offer religious instruction or language immersion, and care provided by trusted community members in their homes.
This initiative is expected to reshape care for over 45,000 children across New Mexico. However, its influence is spilling beyond state borders, with other governments in New York and Connecticut expressing interest in similar programs.
The New Mexico state government claims the program will save families approximately $12,000 annually per child, but it’s projected to cost the state around $463 million in fiscal year 2026, escalating to $606 million in fiscal year 2027. Funding will come from the Early Childhood Education and Care Fund, which has been supported by revenue from oil and gas taxes and federal mineral leasing, and was initially seeded with $300 million from the state’s general fund. A referendum approved by over 70% of voters in 2022 allocated a portion of revenue from the Land Grant Permanent Fund—used to finance public services—to early childhood education.
In April 2025, Grisham signed legislation doubling the annual amount sent from this fund to the child care program from $250 million to $500 million, with SB241 allowing for up to $700 million to be withdrawn total from the fund through 2031.
Critics like Chelsea Follett of the Cato Institute argue the program may unnecessarily shift costs to taxpayers without ensuring the promised delivery of child care.
Currently, only 10.8 million child care spots are available in the U.S., while 14.8 million children under five are in need of child care, indicating a significant shortfall. In New Mexico, about 20.5% of children under five in need of childcare are unable to secure spots.
To ameliorate this gap, the New Mexico Early Childhood Education and Care Department forecasts that the state must establish an additional 55 licensed child care centers and 120 licensed homes, among other initiatives.
State efforts include providing low-interest loans for creating new facilities and technical assistance for early childhood professionals. The state has also relaxed zoning restrictions to promote the development of child care centers.
Experts like Jessica Brown from the University of South Carolina indicate that while it takes time for supply to catch up with demand, new centers are opening at an increasing rate, which bodes well for the future of child care availability in New Mexico.
The state has introduced financial incentives to uplift care quality as well, linking additional subsidies to child care quality ratings established through the University of New Mexico’s Early Childhood Service Center. However, challenges remain in maintaining service quality across the program, given the complexity of ensuring enough qualified staff and trained individuals.
Legislators are also working to counter potential labor shortages in child care by incentivizing wage increases. Full-time providers that pay entry-level staff between $18 to $21 per hour based on facility ratings are eligible for extra subsidies, with national median wages for childcare workers currently around $15.41 per hour.
Efforts to increase wages for child care workers are seen as pivotal to making the profession more attractive. Follett warns that just raising salaries might not drive quality improvements as anticipated.
